content.text: Outpainting extends an existing image beyond its original boundaries by generating contextually coherent new content in the surrounding area. Where inpainting fills a hole inside an image, outpainting expands the canvas outward — revealing a wider field of view, extending a background, or converting an image from one aspect ratio to another. Under the hood, outpainting is implemented using the same diffusion inpainting pipeline: the original image is pasted onto a larger canvas, a binary mask marks the empty padded regions as the generation target, and the model fills them in guided by a text prompt.

Outpainting is the task of generating plausible image content in a region that lies outside the boundary of a source image. The model must extrapolate the scene — continuing lighting, perspective, texture, and subject matter — while staying semantically consistent with the original content and a guiding text prompt. This is fundamentally harder than inpainting: the model has no pixel information in the target region at all, so it must reason about what would naturally surround the given crop.

Diffusion-based outpainting treats canvas expansion as a special case of inpainting. The source image is placed on a larger canvas with neutral filler (gray, noise, or a mirrored edge) in the new region. A white mask covers the padded area. The inpainting model then generates content under the mask while the original pixels, which fall under the black mask region, are recomposed from the source at each denoising step. The result is a wider image where the original content is pixel-perfect and the generated extension blends at the boundary.

Images are captured with a fixed frame, but the intended use case often demands a different aspect ratio or a wider scene. Cropping loses content; padding with solid fills looks unprofessional. Outpainting solves this by synthesizing believable continuations of the scene. A square photograph can become a 16:9 cinematic frame for a video header. A portrait can gain a richer background. A product shot can receive an extended studio environment without re-shooting.

The technique also unlocks creative workflows. Artists use iterative outpainting to explore what might exist outside a reference crop — building large panoramic scenes tile by tile. Concept designers extend early sketches into full environment art. Film and TV post-production teams use AI outpainting to extend practical sets digitally. Each of these workflows would otherwise require re-rendering or re-shooting, making outpainting a major time and cost saver.
— change a square image to 16:9, 4:3, or any target ratio without cropping existing content.
— extend studio product backgrounds, nature scenes, or interior environments beyond the original frame.
— iteratively expand an image left and right to create wide landscape panoramas from a single seed image.
— reveal implied content outside a tight crop, such as the rest of an architectural space or a wider landscape view.
— use iterative outpainting as a generative ideation tool, growing an image outward in multiple passes to discover the surrounding world.
— the source pixels are pasted back after each denoising pass, so the original region remains pixel-perfect.
— without feathered mask edges, a sharp discontinuity appears at the boundary between original and generated regions. Gaussian blurring the mask mitigates this but does not eliminate it entirely.
— each iterative step only sees a local crop of the canvas. Over many steps the generated content can drift in style, lighting, or subject matter relative to the original.
— each expansion step is a full inference pass. A 4× expansion with overlapping 25% steps requires 12–16 passes, which takes several minutes on a GPU.
— the model may not maintain a coherent vanishing point or camera perspective when extending architectural or structured scenes.
— the quality and coherence of the extension depends heavily on how accurately the prompt describes the existing scene. Poor prompts produce generic or mismatched content.

Outpainting reuses the inpainting framework. The key mathematical insight is that the inpainting model is conditioned on both the masked image latent and the binary mask, allowing it to generate coherent content under the white mask region while treating the black-masked region as fixed context. For outpainting, the entire padded area (the new canvas extension) is set to white in the mask, and the original image region is set to black.
of size H × W, and an expansion of E pixels to the right, the padded canvas
has size H × (W + E). The mask
is zero on the original region and one on the padded region:

The inpainting U-Net takes a 9-channel input at each denoising step: 4 channels of noisy latent, 4 channels of the VAE-encoded masked canvas, and 1 channel of the downscaled mask. The model predicts the noise to remove from the padded region while the original region's latent is recomposed from the noise-scheduled version of the original at each step. At
(the final denoising step), the generated latent for the padded region is VAE-decoded to pixel space, and the original pixels are pasted back exactly over the decoded output in the original region.
The composite output at each denoising step is:
is the original latent forward-diffused to noise level t, and
is the model's predicted denoised latent. The mask
is downscaled to latent resolution (8× smaller than pixel resolution) for this operation.

A hard binary mask (0 or 255) creates a sharp boundary in the generation target that often produces a visible seam. Applying a Gaussian blur to the mask edge creates a soft transition zone where both the original latent and the generated latent contribute, weighted by the mask value. With a blur radius of 8–20 pixels, the transition appears as a gradual blend rather than a sharp line. The blur is applied before passing the mask to the pipeline, and the original pixels are always pasted back hard-edged over the output at the end to guarantee exact preservation.

attrs.alt: Outpainting pipeline: original image is placed on a padded canvas, binary mask covers the new region (white=generate, black=keep), 9-channel inpaint U-Net generates the extension, original pixels are pasted back and boundary is feathered; iterative loop repeats for large expansions

content.text: The pipeline has four stages. First, the original image is placed on a larger canvas using PIL, with the padded area filled with a neutral gray (128, 128, 128) or a mirrored edge. Second, a binary mask image marks the padded area white (generate) and the original area black (keep). Third, the inpainting pipeline processes the padded canvas and mask identically to any inpainting task. Fourth, the original pixels are pasted back at pixel accuracy and the mask edge blur provides the feathered boundary blend. For iterative outpainting, the full extended output becomes the new original and the process repeats.
Outpainting is the right choice when you need to expand an image's visible area without modifying any existing content. Use it for:
Aspect ratio changes for publication or display (portrait → landscape, square → 16:9).
Background extension for e-commerce product photography where the subject must remain unchanged.
Generative panorama creation — iteratively expanding left and right from a seed image to build wide landscapes.
Concept art workflows where an initial sketch needs an expanded environment built around it.
You need spatially accurate extensions (the model may not preserve exact perspective or geometry).
The expansion factor is very large (>2× per step) — break it into incremental 25–40% steps instead.
The scene has strong structural regularity (grids, tiled floors, text) where the model will likely break the pattern.

The first step in any outpainting pipeline is creating the padded canvas and corresponding mask. PIL provides all necessary primitives. The key decisions are: which direction to expand, how much to expand (in pixels), and what to fill the padded area with before inpainting. Gray fill (128, 128, 128) is neutral and safe. Mirror-pad fill (flipping the edge region) can sometimes improve edge blending for textures.

from PIL import Image, ImageFilter, ImageDraw
import numpy as np

def create_outpaint_inputs(
    image: Image.Image,
    direction: str,        # "right", "left", "bottom", "top"
    expand_px: int,        # pixels to add in the expansion direction
    feather_radius: int = 12,  # Gaussian blur radius for mask edge
) -> tuple[Image.Image, Image.Image]:
    """Return (padded_canvas, feathered_mask) for outpainting."""
    W, H = image.size

    if direction == "right":
        new_size = (W + expand_px, H)
        paste_at = (0, 0)
    elif direction == "left":
        new_size = (W + expand_px, H)
        paste_at = (expand_px, 0)
    elif direction == "bottom":
        new_size = (W, H + expand_px)
        paste_at = (0, 0)
    elif direction == "top":
        new_size = (W, H + expand_px)
        paste_at = (0, expand_px)
    else:
        raise ValueError(f"Unknown direction: {direction}")

    # 1. Build padded canvas (neutral gray fill)
    canvas = Image.new("RGB", new_size, (128, 128, 128))
    canvas.paste(image, paste_at)

    # 2. Build binary mask: 255 = generate, 0 = keep
    px, py = paste_at
    mask_hard = Image.new("L", new_size, 255)    # all white
    draw = ImageDraw.Draw(mask_hard)
    # Original region → black (preserve)
    draw.rectangle([px, py, px + W - 1, py + H - 1], fill=0)

    # 3. Feather the mask edge with Gaussian blur
    mask_soft = mask_hard.filter(ImageFilter.GaussianBlur(radius=feather_radius))

    return canvas, mask_soft


# Usage
image = Image.open("photo.jpg").convert("RGB").resize((512, 512))
canvas, mask = create_outpaint_inputs(image, direction="right", expand_px=256)
print(canvas.size)   # (768, 512)
print(mask.size)     # (768, 512)

With the canvas and mask in hand, running the inpainting pipeline is straightforward. Most SD 1.5 and SDXL inpainting pipelines accept arbitrary input sizes provided they are divisible by 8 (for SD 1.5) or 64 (for SDXL). For large canvas sizes, resize to the nearest valid multiple or use tiled VAE decoding.

import torch
from diffusers import StableDiffusionInpaintPipeline
from PIL import Image

pipe = StableDiffusionInpaintPipeline.from_pretrained(
    "runwayml/stable-diffusion-inpainting",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
).to("cuda")
pipe.enable_attention_slicing()

def outpaint(
    image: Image.Image,
    prompt: str,
    direction: str = "right",
    expand_px: int = 256,
    num_steps: int = 40,
    guidance_scale: float = 7.5,
    strength: float = 1.0,
    feather: int = 12,
    seed: int = 42,
) -> Image.Image:
    W, H = image.size

    canvas, mask = create_outpaint_inputs(image, direction, expand_px, feather)
    new_w, new_h = canvas.size

    # SD 1.5 inpainting works best at sizes divisible by 8
    proc_w = ((new_w + 7) // 8) * 8
    proc_h = ((new_h + 7) // 8) * 8

    canvas_in = canvas.resize((proc_w, proc_h))
    mask_in   = mask.resize((proc_w, proc_h))

    generator = torch.Generator("cuda").manual_seed(seed)
    result = pipe(
        prompt=prompt,
        negative_prompt="blurry, low quality, distorted, watermark",
        image=canvas_in,
        mask_image=mask_in,
        num_inference_steps=num_steps,
        guidance_scale=guidance_scale,
        strength=strength,
        generator=generator,
    ).images[0]

    # Resize output back to actual canvas size
    result = result.resize((new_w, new_h))

    # Paste original pixels back exactly (guarantees exact preservation)
    if direction == "right":   paste_at = (0, 0)
    elif direction == "left":  paste_at = (expand_px, 0)
    elif direction == "bottom": paste_at = (0, 0)
    elif direction == "top":   paste_at = (0, expand_px)
    result.paste(image, paste_at)

    return result


# Expand a landscape photo to the right by 256 pixels
src = Image.open("landscape.jpg").convert("RGB").resize((512, 512))
prompt = "wide aerial mountain landscape, blue sky, pine forest, photorealistic"
output = outpaint(src, prompt, direction="right", expand_px=256)
output.save("landscape_extended.png")

Expanding a single direction once is rarely sufficient. For large expansions, breaking the step into multiple smaller increments (25–40% of the current image width per step) produces much better coherence than trying to generate a large blank region in one pass. The model has more context to work from in each small step, reducing hallucination.

For expanding in all four directions — to produce a wider and taller scene from a small seed — the safest approach is to process one direction at a time. Expanding right, then left, then bottom, then top avoids the corner ambiguity where two generated regions must agree at a single point.

def iterative_outpaint(
    image: Image.Image,
    prompt: str,
    direction: str = "right",
    total_expand_px: int = 768,
    step_px: int = 192,          # 25% of 768
    pipe=pipe,
    **kwargs,
) -> Image.Image:
    """Expand iteratively in step_px increments."""
    current = image
    remaining = total_expand_px
    step_num = 0

    while remaining > 0:
        this_step = min(step_px, remaining)
        print(f"Step {step_num}: expanding {direction} by {this_step}px")
        current = outpaint(current, prompt, direction=direction,
                           expand_px=this_step, **kwargs)
        remaining -= this_step
        step_num += 1

    return current


def four_direction_outpaint(
    image: Image.Image,
    prompt: str,
    expand_each: int = 384,     # pixels to add per direction
    step_px: int = 128,
    pipe=pipe,
    **kwargs,
) -> Image.Image:
    """Expand the image in all four directions."""
    current = image
    for direction in ["right", "left", "bottom", "top"]:
        print(f"Expanding {direction}...")
        current = iterative_outpaint(
            current, prompt,
            direction=direction,
            total_expand_px=expand_each,
            step_px=step_px,
            pipe=pipe,
            **kwargs,
        )
    return current


# Grow a 512×512 seed into a ~1280×1280 scene
seed = Image.open("forest_seed.jpg").convert("RGB").resize((512, 512))
prompt = "dense ancient forest, morning mist, tall oak trees, mossy ground, soft light"
wide_scene = four_direction_outpaint(seed, prompt, expand_each=384, step_px=128)
wide_scene.save("forest_panorama.png")

SDXL inpainting produces significantly better quality for outpainting because it generates at 1024×1024 natively and has a better understanding of natural scene composition. The API is nearly identical to SD 1.5 inpainting, but you should use 1024-pixel base dimensions and can pass

from diffusers import StableDiffusionXLInpaintPipeline
import torch

sdxl_pipe = StableDiffusionXLInpaintPipeline.from_pretrained(
    "diffusers/stable-diffusion-xl-1.0-inpainting-0.1",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
    variant="fp16",
).to("cuda")
sdxl_pipe.enable_model_cpu_offload()

def sdxl_outpaint(
    image: Image.Image,
    prompt: str,
    direction: str = "right",
    expand_px: int = 512,
    num_steps: int = 50,
    guidance_scale: float = 8.0,
    strength: float = 0.99,
    seed: int = 0,
) -> Image.Image:
    W, H = image.size
    canvas, mask = create_outpaint_inputs(image, direction, expand_px, feather_radius=20)
    new_w, new_h = canvas.size

    # SDXL works best at multiples of 64
    proc_w = ((new_w + 63) // 64) * 64
    proc_h = ((new_h + 63) // 64) * 64

    canvas_in = canvas.resize((proc_w, proc_h))
    mask_in   = mask.resize((proc_w, proc_h))

    generator = torch.Generator("cuda").manual_seed(seed)
    result = sdxl_pipe(
        prompt=prompt,
        negative_prompt="blurry, artifacts, low quality, ugly",
        image=canvas_in,
        mask_image=mask_in,
        num_inference_steps=num_steps,
        guidance_scale=guidance_scale,
        strength=strength,
        original_size=(proc_h, proc_w),
        target_size=(proc_h, proc_w),
        generator=generator,
    ).images[0]

    result = result.resize((new_w, new_h))
    # Paste exact original pixels back
    paste_at = {
        "right": (0, 0), "left": (expand_px, 0),
        "bottom": (0, 0), "top": (0, expand_px),
    }[direction]
    result.paste(image, paste_at)
    return result


# SDXL outpaint: expand a 1024×1024 image to 1536×1024
orig = Image.open("cityscape.jpg").convert("RGB").resize((1024, 1024))
prompt = "wide city skyline at dusk, glass towers, reflective water, golden hour"
result = sdxl_outpaint(orig, prompt, direction="right", expand_px=512)
result.save("cityscape_wide.png")
Real-world Example: Aspect Ratio Expansion for Social Media

A common production scenario is taking a 1:1 square photograph and expanding it to a 16:9 landscape for a YouTube thumbnail or website banner. The subject stays centered and unchanged; content is generated on the left and right flanks. This is more cost-effective and visually consistent than trying to re-shoot in the target format or using solid color fills.

from PIL import Image
from pathlib import Path

def square_to_16x9(
    image_path: str,
    prompt: str,
    output_path: str,
    pipe=pipe,
    seed: int = 42,
) -> Image.Image:
    """
    Convert a square image to 16:9 by outpainting left and right equally.
    Input:  W × W square
    Output: (W * 16/9) × W  ≈ 1.78×W wide
    """
    image = Image.open(image_path).convert("RGB")
    W, H = image.size
    assert W == H, "Input must be square"

    # 16:9 target width
    target_w = int(W * 16 / 9)
    side_expand = (target_w - W) // 2

    print(f"Expanding {W}×{H} → {target_w}×{H}")
    print(f"Adding {side_expand}px left and {side_expand}px right")

    # Expand in small steps: 25% of W per step
    step = max(64, W // 4)
    current = image

    # Right side
    current = iterative_outpaint(current, prompt, direction="right",
                                  total_expand_px=side_expand, step_px=step,
                                  pipe=pipe, seed=seed)
    # Left side (image has grown by side_expand to the right)
    current = iterative_outpaint(current, prompt, direction="left",
                                  total_expand_px=side_expand, step_px=step,
                                  pipe=pipe, seed=seed + 1)
    current.save(output_path)
    print(f"Saved to {output_path}: {current.size}")
    return current


# Convert a 512×512 portrait photo to 16:9 banner
square_to_16x9(
    "portrait_512.jpg",
    prompt="professional studio background, soft bokeh, neutral gray tones, elegant",
    output_path="portrait_16x9_banner.png",
    seed=7,
)

In production at scale (e.g., batch-processing thousands of product images for an e-commerce catalog), this pipeline runs on multiple GPUs in parallel. Each image is processed independently, with the prompt templated from product metadata (e.g.,
). Throughput on an A100 GPU is roughly 15–20 images per minute for 512-pixel SD 1.5 outpainting at 40 steps.

A binary 0/255 mask with no Gaussian blur creates a visible seam at the boundary. The generated content and the original pixels meet at a hard edge that is often detectable even in otherwise good outputs. Always apply a blur radius of at least 8 pixels on the mask before passing it to the pipeline.

# Wrong: hard binary mask
mask_hard = Image.new("L", canvas.size, 255)
ImageDraw.Draw(mask_hard).rectangle(original_box, fill=0)
# This creates a sharp seam at the boundary

# Right: feathered mask
mask_soft = mask_hard.filter(ImageFilter.GaussianBlur(radius=12))
# Now the transition zone is ~24 px wide; the model blends smoothly
Expanding too far in a single step

Generating a large new region (>50% of the original image width) in a single pass causes coherence failures. The model has too little context relative to the generation target and often fills the space with generic or unrelated content. Use iterative expansion with step sizes of 20–35% of the current image width.

# Wrong: single-step expansion of 100% width
result = outpaint(image, prompt, direction="right", expand_px=512)  # image is 512 wide
# Model fills a 512px gap with no context on its right boundary → hallucinations

# Right: iterative 25% steps
result = iterative_outpaint(image, prompt, direction="right",
                             total_expand_px=512, step_px=128)
# 4 steps of 128px each; each step has ~80% context coverage
Not pasting original pixels back after generation

The feathered mask means the VAE decode operation slightly blurs the original region. If you use the raw pipeline output without pasting the original pixels back, the original content will be subtly degraded — especially visible in text, faces, or high-frequency detail. Always paste the source image back over the output after resizing.

# Wrong: use pipeline output directly
output = pipe(prompt=prompt, image=canvas, mask_image=mask).images[0]
# Original region is slightly degraded due to VAE encode/decode cycle

# Right: paste original back at pixel precision
output = pipe(prompt=prompt, image=canvas, mask_image=mask).images[0]
output = output.resize((new_w, new_h))   # back to actual canvas size
output.paste(image, paste_at)            # exact source pixels over the output

gives the model no guidance about what should appear in the expanded region. For a forest photo, the model might generate a city skyline. Write a prompt that describes both the original content and what you want the extension to contain, using the same style keywords as the original image.
— keep each expansion step between 20% and 30% of the current image dimension. This gives the model enough context (70–80% known) to extrapolate convincingly.
— use larger feather radii for landscape or nature scenes (soft textures tolerate blending) and smaller radii for structured scenes with defined edges.

— include the dominant colors, lighting condition, season, and setting from the original in the prompt. For a snowy mountain photo: "alpine landscape, snow-covered peaks, overcast sky, conifer forest, photorealistic".
Fix the seed per expansion run
— using a fixed seed per direction ensures reproducible results when iterating on prompt changes.
— outpainting benefits from 40–50 inference steps (vs. 25–30 for inpainting). The model has less visual context and needs more steps to produce coherent detail in the new region.

— neutral gray fill (128, 128, 128) is the safest default. Mirror-pad (reflecting the image edge) can sometimes improve coherence for textures but may cause visible reflection artifacts for structured scenes.
— for outpainting (pure generation in a new region), use strength close to 1.0. Lower strengths introduce undesired influence from the gray fill in the padded area.

Each outpainting step is one full diffusion inference pass. For SD 1.5 at 512 px with 40 steps, a single expansion takes approximately 3–5 seconds on an RTX 3090. Iterative 4× expansion (left and right) with 25% steps requires approximately 24 passes, taking 80–120 seconds total. SDXL at 1024 px is 4–6× slower per step.

For batch processing, multiple images can be processed in parallel across multiple GPUs without dependency conflicts. Within a single GPU, pipeline component reuse (load once, call many times) is essential — loading the model for each image would dominate total runtime. Memory consumption during outpainting is the same as standard inpainting:
reduce peak VRAM for large canvas sizes.
Several tools expose outpainting capabilities, either through dedicated APIs or as extensions of their inpainting pipeline:
— no dedicated outpainting pipeline; implement via canvas padding +
— built-in Outpainting extension under img2img tab. Supports Poor Man's Outpainting and Differential Outpainting modes with canvas resize UI.
— OpenAI's edit endpoint supports outpainting via mask images. RGBA PNG with transparent padding region acts as the mask.
— Photoshop's native AI outpainting tool (Firefly-powered). Supports multi-direction expansion with interactive crop handles.
— "Zoom Out 2×" and custom zoom options for outpainting a generation; outputs a zoomed-out version with generated border content.
— node-based pipeline builder supports custom outpainting workflows including tiled and iterative variants with full control over each component.
Good for textures, poor for objects
Upscale then crop to target size
Moderate; GAN artifacts in large regions

Outpainting inherits all content safety responsibilities of the underlying inpainting model. When the padded region is generated from a prompt, the NSFW safety checker and content filters of the pipeline apply to the generated region. However, a subtlety arises: the original image may be safe while the prompt-guided extension introduces unsafe content adjacent to the original. The safety checker must therefore evaluate the
, not just the generated region in isolation.

A practical concern for production systems is copyright: if the original image is copyrighted, outpainting creates a derivative work. Depending on jurisdiction and use case, the license of the original may not permit derivative works. Always use images you own or have rights to modify. For AI-generated seed images, outpainting derivatives are generally considered owned by the user under most platform terms of service, but this is jurisdiction-dependent.

For iterative outpainting systems that run unattended, build in a safety gate that runs a classifier (such as the built-in SD safety checker or a dedicated NSFW classifier) on each output before it is accepted as the input for the next step. A single step producing inappropriate content should halt the pipeline rather than allowing the content to propagate through subsequent expansions where it would grow in size.
